revision.text:
{{infobox religious building |name=Pyarumizu-utaki |image=Miyako harimizu utaki.jpg }} '''Pyarumizu-utaki''' (漲水御嶽, Harimizu Utaki, [[Miyako language]]: Piyarimizu Utaki) is an {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽|12=draft}} located in Hirara Nishizato, {{ill|Miyakojima, Okinawa|ja|宮古島市|12=draft}}, [[Okinawa Prefecture]]. It is also known as "Tsukasayā"<ref name="okinawastory">[http://www.okinawastory.jp/spot/20340905 Harimizu Utaki] Okinawa Story (Okinawa Convention & Visitors Bureau)</ref>. {{coord|24|48|26.49|N|125|16|46.58|E|scale:2500|display=title}}
== Overview == Harimizu Utaki enshrines the two deities, Koi-tsuno (古意角) and Koitama (姑依玉). It has been a site of worship since before the establishment of the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]] (1429) and is the setting for various [[myths]] and [[legends]] related to the creation of Miyako Island<ref name="okinawastory" />.
== Origin and Legends ==
=== Creation Myth === {{See also|Miyako Island#Myths}} After Miyako Island was formed, the deities '''Koi-tsuno''' and '''Koitama''' descended from the heavens to Harimizu, accompanied by many other gods. After creating humans and all things on earth, they returned to the heavens. It is said that by establishing the Utaki and continuing worship, the island prospered and flourished<ref name="minwa">[http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/chiiki_ritou/simajima/minwa.html#15 The Story of the Islands: Miyako Island's Beginning] Okinawa Prefectural Government</ref><ref>Imabayashi (2015)</ref>.
=== Human-Serpent Marriage Legend === In Hirara, a noble couple lived but had no children. They prayed to the heavens and were granted a beautiful daughter. When she became pregnant at a young age, her parents inquired, and she revealed that a young man visited her each night. The father traced the man's whereabouts and discovered he was a giant serpent living in the cave at Harimizu Utaki.
That night, the serpent appeared in the daughter's dream, revealing himself as Koi-tsuno, the guardian deity of the island. He instructed her to bring their three daughters to the Utaki when they turned three years old. The daughters were brought there, and as they embraced the serpent without fear, he wept with joy and transformed them into guardian deities of the island. The serpent ascended to the heavens, radiating light.
=== Historical Records === According to "Miyakojima Kijishitsugi," a mid-18th-century compilation of myths and folklore from Miyako Island, Koi-tsuno requested the Heavenly Emperor to become the island's guardian deity. The Heavenly Emperor blessed him, broke off the tip of the [[Ama-no-Iwato]] and cast it into the ocean, forming Miyako Island. Koi-tsuno then descended with the goddess Koitama to the site of the present-day Utaki, where they gave birth to many gods and ensured the prosperity of the island's people<ref>{{DOClink|[http://www.kisc.meiji.ac.jp/~meikodai/datebasa/miyakojima_kijisitsugi.doc Miyakojima Kijishitsugi]}} Meiji University Institute of Japanese Ancient Studies</ref>.
== History == According to "Chūdō-shi Honke Puku," the stone walls on the southern side were built by [[Nakasone Toyomiya]] after his victory over [[Oyake Akahachi]] in the [[1500 Oyake Akahachi Rebellion]], fulfilling his vow to enshrine the spirits that aided his success<ref name="shiseki">[http://www.city.miyakojima.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/miyakojimabunkazai/shiseki.html Historic Sites 1-25] Miyakojima City</ref>.
On [[December 2, 2017]], the deteriorating [[torii]] was replaced, and a completion tier 2]][[Category: ceremony was held<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on New Torii Reconstructed for Harimizu Utaki] Miyako Shinpo, December 5, 2017</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />. * Harimizu Stone-Paved Road - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Imabayashi, Naoki (2015). "Geography, Folklore, and History of the Sakishima Islands: Miyako and Yaeyama Islands." == See Also == * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] - [[Religious Beliefs in Ryukyu]]
== External Links == * [https://miyakojimabunkazai.jp/bunkazaiinfo738/ Miyakojima City Cultural Properties: Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls]

revision.text:
[[ファイル:Miyako harimizu utaki.jpg|thumb|right|280px|漲水御嶽]] '''漲水御嶽'''（はりみずうたき、[[宮古方言]]：ぴゃるみずうたき）は、[[沖縄県]][[宮古島市]]平良字西里にある[[御嶽 (沖縄)|御嶽]]。別名「ツカサヤー」<ref name="okinawastory">[http://www.okinawastory.jp/spot/20340905 漲水御嶽] おきなわ物語（沖縄観光コンベンションビューロー）</ref>。 {{coord|24|48|26.49|N|125|16|46.58|E|scale:2500|display=title}}
== 概要 == 古意角（コイツノ）と姑依玉（コイタマ）の男女二神を祀る。[[琉球王国]]建国（[[1429年]]）以前から信仰を集めている御嶽であり、宮古島の創生神話をはじめとする多くの[[神話]]と[[伝説]]の舞台でもある<ref name="okinawastory" />。
== 由来、伝説 ==
=== 創世神話 === {{See also|宮古島#神話}} 宮古島が作られた後、天より、'''古意角'''（'''恋角'''）という神と'''姑依玉'''（'''恋玉'''）という女神が、多くの神々を従え漲水に降り立った。人間と天地の万物一切を作ったあと天に戻った。ここに御嶽を立ててよく拝み、島は大いに賑わい栄えたと言う伝説である。<ref name="minwa">[http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/chiiki_ritou/simajima/minwa.html#15 島々のはなし 14 宮古島 宮古島の始まり] 知・旅・住 離島総合情報サイト 沖縄のしまじま（沖縄県）</ref><ref>今林（2015）</ref>。
=== 人蛇婚伝説 === 平良の住屋に貴人の夫婦が住んでいたが子供がなかったため、授かるよう天に祈ったところ、美しい娘を授かる。若くして身籠ったため両親が問うと、若い男が毎晩[[夜這い]]で閨を訪れていたと言う。父が男の居場所を突き止めると、それは漲水のイベ（御嶽の中心部）の洞窟の中に居た大蛇であった。
その晩、身籠った娘の夢枕に大蛇が現れた。大蛇は自らを古意角、島立の神と名乗り、島の守り神を造るために娘のもとに来たと言う。娘は三人の娘を産むので三歳になった時、御嶽に連れてくるように大蛇は告げた。
果たして娘は大蛇の言う通り三人の娘子を産んだ。子供らが三歳になり、御嶽に連れて行くが大蛇の恐ろしさに母は逃げ帰った。子供達は大蛇を恐れる事なくそれぞれ首、胴、尾に抱き付くと、大蛇は涙を流して喜び、子供達を島の守り神に変え御嶽の中へと姿を消した。大蛇（古意角）は光を放ちながら天に昇っていった。
=== 書誌 === [[18世紀]]中頃にまとめられた『宮古島記事仕次』という宮古島の神話・伝説と昔話をまとめた本によれば、地上の守護神となることを古意角が天帝に願い出ると、天帝はこれを祝福し、[[天岩戸|天の岩戸]]の先端を折って大海に投げ入れ宮古島を作ったという。その後、古意角は姑依玉という[[女神]]を伴い現在御嶽のある地に降臨し多くの神々を産み育て、そして人々が繁栄を得た、と記されている<ref>{{DOClink|[http://www.kisc.meiji.ac.jp/~meikodai/datebasa/miyakojima_kijisitsugi.doc 宮古嶋記事仕次]}} 明治大学 日本古代学研究所</ref>。
== 沿革 == 『忠導氏本宗家譜』によれば、南側の石垣は、[[1500年]]の[[オヤケアカハチの乱]]に際して、[[仲宗根豊見親]]が、神霊の加護で[[オヤケアカハチ]]を征討した暁には神域を整備することを警願し、戦に勝利した後に築いたものとされる<ref name="shiseki">[http://www.city.miyakojima.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/miyakojimabunkazai/shiseki.html 史跡1～25] 宮古島市</ref>。
[[2017年]]（平成29年）[[12月2日]]には、老朽化した[[鳥居]]を建て替え、落成式が行われている<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on 老朽化で建て替えの漲水御嶽の新鳥居が落成] 宮古新報、2017年12月5日</ref>。
== 文化財 == * 漲水御嶽と石垣 - 宮古島市史跡（1974年（昭和49年）8月29日指定）<ref name="shiseki" /> * 漲水石畳道 - 宮古島市史跡（1974年（昭和49年）8月29日指定）<ref name="shiseki" />
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 ==
* 『先島諸島の地理・民俗・歴史―宮古諸島と八重山諸島―』今林直樹（2015）
== 関連項目 ==
*[[琉球神道]] - [[琉球における信仰]]
== 外部リンク == * [https://miyakojimabunkazai.jp/bunkazaiinfo738/ 市指定：史跡】漲水御嶽と石垣～はりみずうたきといしがき～] 綾道（宮古島市教育委員会公認歴史文化観光ロードアプリサイト）
{{Reli-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:はりみすうたき}} [[Category:御嶽 (沖縄)]] [[Category:宮古島市の建築物]] [[Category:宮古島市の歴史]]

{{infobox religious building |name=Pyarumizu-utaki |image=Miyako harimizu utaki.jpg }} '''Pyarumizu-utaki''' (漲水御嶽, Harimizu Utaki, [[Miyako language]]: Piyarimizu Utaki) is an {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} located in Hirara Nishizato, {{ill|Miyakojima, Okinawa|ja|宮古島市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, [[Okinawa Prefecture]]. The Heavenly Emperor blessed him, broke off the tip of the [[Ama-no-Iwato]] and cast it into the ocean, forming Miyako Island. Koi-tsuno then descended with the goddess Koitama to the site of the present-day Utaki, where they gave birth to many gods and ensured the prosperity of the island's people<ref>{{DOClink|[http://www.kisc.meiji.ac.jp/~meikodai/datebasa/miyakojima_kijisitsugi.doc Miyakojima Kijishitsugi]}} Meiji University Institute of Japanese Ancient Studies</ref>.
== History == According to "Chūdō-shi Honke Puku," the stone walls on the southern side were built by [[Nakasone Toyomiya]] after his victory over [[Oyake Akahachi]] in the [[1500 Oyake Akahachi Rebellion]], fulfilling his vow to enshrine the spirits that aided his success<ref name="shiseki">[http://www.city.miyakojima.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/miyakojimabunkazai/shiseki.html Historic Sites 1-25] Miyakojima City</ref>.
On [[December 2, 2017]], the deteriorating [[torii]] was replaced, and a Completion tier 2]][[Category: ceremony was held<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on New Torii Reconstructed for Harimizu Utaki] Miyako Shinpo, December 5, 2017</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />. * Harimizu Stone-Paved Road - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />.

{{infobox religious building |name=Pyarumizu-utaki |image=Miyako harimizu utaki.jpg }} '''Pyarumizu-utaki''' (漲水御嶽, Harimizu Utaki, [[Miyako language]]: Piyarimizu Utaki) is an {{ill|Utaki|ja|御嶽|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utaki|qq=}} located in Hirara Nishizato, {{ill|Miyakojima, Okinawa|ja|宮古島市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyakojima, Okinawa|qq=}}, [[Okinawa Prefecture]]. It is also known as "Tsukasayā"<ref name="okinawastory">[http://www.okinawastory.jp/spot/20340905 Harimizu Utaki] Okinawa Story (Okinawa Convention & Visitors Bureau)</ref>. {{coord|24|48|26.49|N|125|16|46.58|E|scale:2500|display=title}}
== Overview == Harimizu Utaki enshrines the two deities, Koi-tsuno (古意角) and Koitama (姑依玉). It has been a site of worship since before the establishment of the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]] (1429) and is the setting for various [[myths]] and [[legends]] related to the creation of Miyako Island<ref name="okinawastory" />.
== Origin and Legends ==
=== Creation Myth === {{See also|Miyako Island#Myths}} After Miyako Island was formed, the deities '''Koi-tsuno''' and '''Koitama''' descended from the heavens to Harimizu, accompanied by many other gods. After creating humans and all things on earth, they returned to the heavens. It is said that by establishing the Utaki and continuing worship, the island prospered and flourished<ref name="minwa">[http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/chiiki_ritou/simajima/minwa.html#15 The Story of the Islands: Miyako Island's Beginning] Okinawa Prefectural Government</ref><ref>Imabayashi (2015)</ref>.
=== Human-Serpent Marriage Legend === In Hirara, a noble couple lived but had no children. They prayed to the heavens and were granted a beautiful daughter. When she became pregnant at a young age, her parents inquired, and she revealed that a young man visited her each night. The father traced the man's whereabouts and discovered he was a giant serpent living in the cave at Harimizu Utaki.
That night, the serpent appeared in the daughter's dream, revealing himself as Koi-tsuno, the guardian deity of the island. He instructed her to bring their three daughters to the Utaki when they turned three years old. The daughters were brought there, and as they embraced the serpent without fear, he wept with joy and transformed them into guardian deities of the island. The serpent ascended to the heavens, radiating light.
=== Historical Records === According to "Miyakojima Kijishitsugi," a mid-18th-century compilation of myths and folklore from Miyako Island, Koi-tsuno requested the Heavenly Emperor to become the island's guardian deity. The Heavenly Emperor blessed him, broke off the tip of the [[Ama-no-Iwato]] and cast it into the ocean, forming Miyako Island. Koi-tsuno then descended with the goddess Koitama to the site of the present-day Utaki, where they gave birth to many gods and ensured the prosperity of the island's people<ref>{{DOClink|[http://www.kisc.meiji.ac.jp/~meikodai/datebasa/miyakojima_kijisitsugi.doc Miyakojima Kijishitsugi]}} Meiji University Institute of Japanese Ancient Studies</ref>.
== History == According to "Chūdō-shi Honke Puku," the stone walls on the southern side were built by [[Nakasone Toyomiya]] after his victory over [[Oyake Akahachi]] in the [[1500 Oyake Akahachi Rebellion]], fulfilling his vow to enshrine the spirits that aided his success<ref name="shiseki">[http://www.city.miyakojima.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/miyakojimabunkazai/shiseki.html Historic Sites 1-25] Miyakojima City</ref>.
On [[December 2, 2017]], the deteriorating [[torii]] was replaced, and a Completion tier 2]][[Category: ceremony was held<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on New Torii Reconstructed for Harimizu Utaki] Miyako Shinpo, December 5, 2017</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />. * Harimizu Stone-Paved Road - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Imabayashi, Naoki (2015). "Geography, Folklore, and History of the Sakishima Islands: Miyako and Yaeyama Islands." == See Also == * [[Ryukyu Shinto]] - [[Religious Beliefs in Ryukyu]]
== External Links == * [https://miyakojimabunkazai.jp/bunkazaiinfo738/ Miyakojima City Cultural Properties: Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls]

revision.text:
{{infobox religious building |name=Pyarumizu-utaki |image=Miyako harimizu utaki.jpg }} '''Pyarumizu-utaki''' (漲水御嶽, Harimizu Utaki, [[Miyako language]]: Piyarimizu Utaki) is an [[:en:Ontake|Utaki]] located in Hirara Nishizato, [[:en:Miyakojima, Okinawa|Miyakojima, Okinawa]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]]. It is also known as "Tsukasayā"<ref name="okinawastory">[http://www.okinawastory.jp/spot/20340905 Harimizu Utaki] Okinawa Story (Okinawa Convention & Visitors Bureau)</ref>. {{coord|24|48|26.49|N|125|16|46.58|E|scale:2500|display=title}}
== Overview == Harimizu Utaki enshrines the two deities, Koi-tsuno (古意角) and Koitama (姑依玉). It has been a site of worship since before the establishment of the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]] (1429) and is the setting for various [[myths]] and [[legends]] related to the creation of Miyako Island<ref name="okinawastory" />.
== Origin and Legends ==
=== Creation Myth === {{See also|Miyako Island#Myths}} After Miyako Island was formed, the deities '''Koi-tsuno''' and '''Koitama''' descended from the heavens to Harimizu, accompanied by many other gods. After creating humans and all things on earth, they returned to the heavens. It is said that by establishing the Utaki and continuing worship, the island prospered and flourished<ref name="minwa">[http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/chiiki_ritou/simajima/minwa.html#15 The Story of the Islands: Miyako Island's Beginning] Okinawa Prefectural Government</ref><ref>Imabayashi (2015)</ref>.
=== Human-Serpent Marriage Legend === In Hirara, a noble couple lived but had no children. They prayed to the heavens and were granted a beautiful daughter. When she became pregnant at a young age, her parents inquired, and she revealed that a young man visited her each night. The father traced the man's whereabouts and discovered he was a giant serpent living in the cave at Harimizu Utaki.
That night, the serpent appeared in the daughter's dream, revealing himself as Koi-tsuno, the guardian deity of the island. He instructed her to bring their three daughters to the Utaki when they turned three years old. The daughters were brought there, and as they embraced the serpent without fear, he wept with joy and transformed them into guardian deities of the island. The serpent ascended to the heavens, radiating light.
=== Historical Records === According to "Miyakojima Kijishitsugi," a mid-18th-century compilation of myths and folklore from Miyako Island, Koi-tsuno requested the Heavenly Emperor to become the island's guardian deity. The Heavenly Emperor blessed him, broke off the tip of the [[Ama-no-Iwato]] and cast it into the ocean, forming Miyako Island. Koi-tsuno then descended with the goddess Koitama to the site of the present-day Utaki, where they gave birth to many gods and ensured the prosperity of the island's people<ref>{{DOClink|[http://www.kisc.meiji.ac.jp/~meikodai/datebasa/miyakojima_kijisitsugi.doc Miyakojima Kijishitsugi]}} Meiji University Institute of Japanese Ancient Studies</ref>.
== History == According to "Chūdō-shi Honke Puku," the stone walls on the southern side were built by [[Nakasone Toyomiya]] after his victory over [[Oyake Akahachi]] in the [[1500 Oyake Akahachi Rebellion]], fulfilling his vow to enshrine the spirits that aided his success<ref name="shiseki">[http://www.city.miyakojima.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/miyakojimabunkazai/shiseki.html Historic Sites 1-25] Miyakojima City</ref>.
On [[December 2, 2017]], the deteriorating [[torii]] was replaced, and a Completion tier 2]][[Category: ceremony was held<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on New Torii Reconstructed for Harimizu Utaki] Miyako Shinpo, December 5, 2017</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />. * Harimizu Stone-Paved Road - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Imabayashi, Naoki (2015). "Geography, Folklore, and History of the Sakishima Islands: Miyako and Yaeyama Islands." == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] - [[Religious Beliefs in Ryukyu]]
== External Links == * [https://miyakojimabunkazai.jp/bunkazaiinfo738/ Miyakojima City Cultural Properties: Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls]

On [[December 2, 2017]], the deteriorating [[torii]] was replaced, and a Completion tier 2]][[Category: ceremony was held<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on New Torii Reconstructed for Harimizu Utaki] Miyako Shinpo, December 5, 2017</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />. * Harimizu Stone-Paved Road - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />.

{{infobox religious building |name=Pyarumizu-utaki |image=Miyako harimizu utaki.jpg }} '''Pyarumizu-utaki''' (漲水御嶽, Harimizu Utaki, [[Miyako language]]: Piyarimizu Utaki) is an [[:en:Ontake|Utaki]] located in Hirara Nishizato, [[:en:Miyakojima, Okinawa|Miyakojima, Okinawa]], [[Okinawa Prefecture]]. It is also known as "Tsukasayā"<ref name="okinawastory">[http://www.okinawastory.jp/spot/20340905 Harimizu Utaki] Okinawa Story (Okinawa Convention & Visitors Bureau)</ref>. {{coord|24|48|26.49|N|125|16|46.58|E|scale:2500|display=title}}
== Overview == Harimizu Utaki enshrines the two deities, Koi-tsuno (古意角) and Koitama (姑依玉). It has been a site of worship since before the establishment of the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]] (1429) and is the setting for various [[myths]] and [[legends]] related to the creation of Miyako Island<ref name="okinawastory" />.
== Origin and Legends ==
=== Creation Myth === {{See also|Miyako Island#Myths}} After Miyako Island was formed, the deities '''Koi-tsuno''' and '''Koitama''' descended from the heavens to Harimizu, accompanied by many other gods. After creating humans and all things on earth, they returned to the heavens. It is said that by establishing the Utaki and continuing worship, the island prospered and flourished<ref name="minwa">[http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/chiiki_ritou/simajima/minwa.html#15 The Story of the Islands: Miyako Island's Beginning] Okinawa Prefectural Government</ref><ref>Imabayashi (2015)</ref>.
=== Human-Serpent Marriage Legend === In Hirara, a noble couple lived but had no children. They prayed to the heavens and were granted a beautiful daughter. When she became pregnant at a young age, her parents inquired, and she revealed that a young man visited her each night. The father traced the man's whereabouts and discovered he was a giant serpent living in the cave at Harimizu Utaki.
That night, the serpent appeared in the daughter's dream, revealing himself as Koi-tsuno, the guardian deity of the island. He instructed her to bring their three daughters to the Utaki when they turned three years old. The daughters were brought there, and as they embraced the serpent without fear, he wept with joy and transformed them into guardian deities of the island. The serpent ascended to the heavens, radiating light.
=== Historical Records === According to "Miyakojima Kijishitsugi," a mid-18th-century compilation of myths and folklore from Miyako Island, Koi-tsuno requested the Heavenly Emperor to become the island's guardian deity. The Heavenly Emperor blessed him, broke off the tip of the [[:en:Ama-no-Iwato|Ama-no-Iwato]] and cast it into the ocean, forming Miyako Island. Koi-tsuno then descended with the goddess Koitama to the site of the present-day Utaki, where they gave birth to many gods and ensured the prosperity of the island's people<ref>{{DOClink|[http://www.kisc.meiji.ac.jp/~meikodai/datebasa/miyakojima_kijisitsugi.doc Miyakojima Kijishitsugi]}} Meiji University Institute of Japanese Ancient Studies</ref>.
== History == According to "Chūdō-shi Honke Puku," the stone walls on the southern side were built by [[Nakasone Toyomiya]] after his victory over [[Oyake Akahachi]] in the [[1500 Oyake Akahachi Rebellion]], fulfilling his vow to enshrine the spirits that aided his success<ref name="shiseki">[http://www.city.miyakojima.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/miyakojimabunkazai/shiseki.html Historic Sites 1-25] Miyakojima City</ref>.
On [[December 2, 2017]], the deteriorating [[torii]] was replaced, and a Completion tier 2]][[Category: ceremony was held<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on New Torii Reconstructed for Harimizu Utaki] Miyako Shinpo, December 5, 2017</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />. * Harimizu Stone-Paved Road - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Imabayashi, Naoki (2015). "Geography, Folklore, and History of the Sakishima Islands: Miyako and Yaeyama Islands." == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] - [[Religious Beliefs in Ryukyu]]
== External Links == * [https://miyakojimabunkazai.jp/bunkazaiinfo738/ Miyakojima City Cultural Properties: Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls]
{{Reli-stub}} {{Okinawan Shrines}}

On [[December 2, 2017]], the deteriorating [[torii]] was replaced, and a Completion tier 2]][[Category: ceremony was held<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on New Torii Reconstructed for Harimizu Utaki] Miyako Shinpo, December 5, 2017</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />. * Harimizu Stone-Paved Road - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Imabayashi, Naoki (2015). "Geography, Folklore, and History of the Sakishima Islands: Miyako and Yaeyama Islands." == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] - [[Religious Beliefs in Ryukyu]]
== External Links == * [https://miyakojimabunkazai.jp/bunkazaiinfo738/ Miyakojima City Cultural Properties: Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls]

It is said that by establishing the Utaki and continuing worship, the island prospered and flourished<ref name="minwa">[http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/chiiki_ritou/simajima/minwa.html#15 The Story of the Islands: Miyako Island's Beginning] Okinawa Prefectural Government</ref><ref>Imabayashi (2015)</ref>.
=== Human-Serpent Marriage Legend === In Hirara, a noble couple lived but had no children. They prayed to the heavens and were granted a beautiful daughter. When she became pregnant at a young age, her parents inquired, and she revealed that a young man visited her each night. The father traced the man's whereabouts and discovered he was a giant serpent living in the cave at Harimizu Utaki.
That night, the serpent appeared in the daughter's dream, revealing himself as Koi-tsuno, the guardian deity of the island. He instructed her to bring their three daughters to the Utaki when they turned three years old. The daughters were brought there, and as they embraced the serpent without fear, he wept with joy and transformed them into guardian deities of the island. The serpent ascended to the heavens, radiating light.
=== Historical Records === According to "Miyakojima Kijishitsugi," a mid-18th-century compilation of myths and folklore from Miyako Island, Koi-tsuno requested the Heavenly Emperor to become the island's guardian deity. The Heavenly Emperor blessed him, broke off the tip of the [[:en:Ama-no-Iwato|Ama-no-Iwato]] and cast it into the ocean, forming Miyako Island. Koi-tsuno then descended with the goddess Koitama to the site of the present-day Utaki, where they gave birth to many gods and ensured the prosperity of the island's people<ref>{{DOClink|[http://www.kisc.meiji.ac.jp/~meikodai/datebasa/miyakojima_kijisitsugi.doc Miyakojima Kijishitsugi]}} Meiji University Institute of Japanese Ancient Studies</ref>.
== History == According to "Chūdō-shi Honke Puku," the stone walls on the southern side were built by [[:en:Nakasone Toyomiya|Nakasone Toyomiya]] after his victory over [[Oyake Akahachi]] in the [[1500 Oyake Akahachi Rebellion]], fulfilling his vow to enshrine the spirits that aided his success<ref name="shiseki">[http://www.city.miyakojima.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/miyakojimabunkazai/shiseki.html Historic Sites 1-25] Miyakojima City</ref>.
On [[December 2, 2017]], the deteriorating [[torii]] was replaced, and a Completion tier 2]][[Category: ceremony was held<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on New Torii Reconstructed for Harimizu Utaki] Miyako Shinpo, December 5, 2017</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />. * Harimizu Stone-Paved Road - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Imabayashi, Naoki (2015). "Geography, Folklore, and History of the Sakishima Islands: Miyako and Yaeyama Islands." == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] - [[Religious Beliefs in Ryukyu]]
== External Links == * [https://miyakojimabunkazai.jp/bunkazaiinfo738/ Miyakojima City Cultural Properties: Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls]

{{infobox religious building |name=Pyarumizu-utaki |image=Miyako harimizu utaki.jpg }} '''Pyarumizu-utaki''' (漲水御嶽, Harimizu Utaki, [[:en:Miyako language|Miyako language]]: Piyarimizu Utaki) is an [[:en:Ontake|Utaki]] located in Hirara Nishizato, [[:en:Miyakojima, Okinawa|Miyakojima, Okinawa]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]]. It is also known as "Tsukasayā"<ref name="okinawastory">[http://www.okinawastory.jp/spot/20340905 Harimizu Utaki] Okinawa Story (Okinawa Convention & Visitors Bureau)</ref>. {{coord|24|48|26.49|N|125|16|46.58|E|scale:2500|display=title}}
== Overview == Harimizu Utaki enshrines the two deities, Koi-tsuno (古意角) and Koitama (姑依玉). It has been a site of worship since before the establishment of the [[Ryukyu Kingdom]] (1429) and is the setting for various [[:en:myths|myths]] and [[:en:legends|legends]] related to the creation of Miyako Island<ref name="okinawastory" />.
== Origin and Legends ==
=== Creation Myth === {{See also|Miyako Island#Myths}} After Miyako Island was formed, the deities '''Koi-tsuno''' and '''Koitama''' descended from the heavens to Harimizu, accompanied by many other gods. After creating humans and all things on earth, they returned to the heavens. It is said that by establishing the Utaki and continuing worship, the island prospered and flourished<ref name="minwa">[http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/chiiki_ritou/simajima/minwa.html#15 The Story of the Islands: Miyako Island's Beginning] Okinawa Prefectural Government</ref><ref>Imabayashi (2015)</ref>.
=== Human-Serpent Marriage Legend === In Hirara, a noble couple lived but had no children. They prayed to the heavens and were granted a beautiful daughter. When she became pregnant at a young age, her parents inquired, and she revealed that a young man visited her each night. The father traced the man's whereabouts and discovered he was a giant serpent living in the cave at Harimizu Utaki.
That night, the serpent appeared in the daughter's dream, revealing himself as Koi-tsuno, the guardian deity of the island. He instructed her to bring their three daughters to the Utaki when they turned three years old. The daughters were brought there, and as they embraced the serpent without fear, he wept with joy and transformed them into guardian deities of the island. The serpent ascended to the heavens, radiating light.
=== Historical Records === According to "Miyakojima Kijishitsugi," a mid-18th-century compilation of myths and folklore from Miyako Island, Koi-tsuno requested the Heavenly Emperor to become the island's guardian deity. The Heavenly Emperor blessed him, broke off the tip of the [[:en:Ama-no-Iwato|Ama-no-Iwato]] and cast it into the ocean, forming Miyako Island. Koi-tsuno then descended with the goddess Koitama to the site of the present-day Utaki, where they gave birth to many gods and ensured the prosperity of the island's people<ref>{{DOClink|[http://www.kisc.meiji.ac.jp/~meikodai/datebasa/miyakojima_kijisitsugi.doc Miyakojima Kijishitsugi]}} Meiji University Institute of Japanese Ancient Studies</ref>.
== History == According to "Chūdō-shi Honke Puku," the stone walls on the southern side were built by [[:en:Nakasone Toyomiya|Nakasone Toyomiya]] after his victory over [[Oyake Akahachi]] in the [[1500 Oyake Akahachi Rebellion]], fulfilling his vow to enshrine the spirits that aided his success<ref name="shiseki">[http://www.city.miyakojima.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/miyakojimabunkazai/shiseki.html Historic Sites 1-25] Miyakojima City</ref>.
On [[December 2, 2017]], the deteriorating [[torii]] was replaced, and a Completion tier 2]][[Category: ceremony was held<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on New Torii Reconstructed for Harimizu Utaki] Miyako Shinpo, December 5, 2017</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />. * Harimizu Stone-Paved Road - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Imabayashi, Naoki (2015). "Geography, Folklore, and History of the Sakishima Islands: Miyako and Yaeyama Islands." == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] - [[Religious Beliefs in Ryukyu]]
== External Links == * [https://miyakojimabunkazai.jp/bunkazaiinfo738/ Miyakojima City Cultural Properties: Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls]

The daughters were brought there, and as they embraced the serpent without fear, he wept with joy and transformed them into guardian deities of the island. The serpent ascended to the heavens, radiating light.
=== Historical Records === According to "Miyakojima Kijishitsugi," a mid-18th-century compilation of myths and folklore from Miyako Island, Koi-tsuno requested the Heavenly Emperor to become the island's guardian deity. The Heavenly Emperor blessed him, broke off the tip of the [[:en:Ama-no-Iwato|Ama-no-Iwato]] and cast it into the ocean, forming Miyako Island. Koi-tsuno then descended with the goddess Koitama to the site of the present-day Utaki, where they gave birth to many gods and ensured the prosperity of the island's people<ref>{{DOClink|[http://www.kisc.meiji.ac.jp/~meikodai/datebasa/miyakojima_kijisitsugi.doc Miyakojima Kijishitsugi]}} Meiji University Institute of Japanese Ancient Studies</ref>.
== History == According to "Chūdō-shi Honke Puku," the stone walls on the southern side were built by [[:en:Nakasone Toyomiya|Nakasone Toyomiya]] after his victory over [[:en:Oyake Akahachi|Oyake Akahachi]] in the [[1500 Oyake Akahachi Rebellion]], fulfilling his vow to enshrine the spirits that aided his success<ref name="shiseki">[http://www.city.miyakojima.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/miyakojimabunkazai/shiseki.html Historic Sites 1-25] Miyakojima City</ref>.
On [[December 2, 2017]], the deteriorating [[torii]] was replaced, and a Completion tier 2]][[Category: ceremony was held<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on New Torii Reconstructed for Harimizu Utaki] Miyako Shinpo, December 5, 2017</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />. * Harimizu Stone-Paved Road - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Imabayashi, Naoki (2015). "Geography, Folklore, and History of the Sakishima Islands: Miyako and Yaeyama Islands." == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] - [[Religious Beliefs in Ryukyu]]
== External Links == * [https://miyakojimabunkazai.jp/bunkazaiinfo738/ Miyakojima City Cultural Properties: Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11565602]] Wikidata name: Harimizu-utaki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox religious building |name=Pyarumizu-utaki |image=Miyako harimizu utaki.jpg }} '''Pyarumizu-utaki''' (漲水御嶽, Harimizu Utaki, [[:en:Miyako language|Miyako language]]: Piyarimizu Utaki) is an [[:en:Ontake|Utaki]] located in Hirara Nishizato, [[:en:Miyakojima, Okinawa|Miyakojima, Okinawa]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]]. It is also known as "Tsukasayā"<ref name="okinawastory">[http://www.okinawastory.jp/spot/20340905 Harimizu Utaki] Okinawa Story (Okinawa Convention & Visitors Bureau)</ref>. {{coord|24|48|26.49|N|125|16|46.58|E|scale:2500|display=title}}
== Overview == Harimizu Utaki enshrines the two deities, Koi-tsuno (古意角) and Koitama (姑依玉). It has been a site of worship since before the establishment of the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]] (1429) and is the setting for various [[:en:myths|myths]] and [[:en:legends|legends]] related to the creation of Miyako Island<ref name="okinawastory" />.
== Origin and Legends ==
=== Creation Myth === {{See also|Miyako Island#Myths}} After Miyako Island was formed, the deities '''Koi-tsuno''' and '''Koitama''' descended from the heavens to Harimizu, accompanied by many other gods. After creating humans and all things on earth, they returned to the heavens. It is said that by establishing the Utaki and continuing worship, the island prospered and flourished<ref name="minwa">[http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/chiiki_ritou/simajima/minwa.html#15 The Story of the Islands: Miyako Island's Beginning] Okinawa Prefectural Government</ref><ref>Imabayashi (2015)</ref>.
=== Human-Serpent Marriage Legend === In Hirara, a noble couple lived but had no children. They prayed to the heavens and were granted a beautiful daughter. When she became pregnant at a young age, her parents inquired, and she revealed that a young man visited her each night. The father traced the man's whereabouts and discovered he was a giant serpent living in the cave at Harimizu Utaki.
That night, the serpent appeared in the daughter's dream, revealing himself as Koi-tsuno, the guardian deity of the island. He instructed her to bring their three daughters to the Utaki when they turned three years old. The daughters were brought there, and as they embraced the serpent without fear, he wept with joy and transformed them into guardian deities of the island. The serpent ascended to the heavens, radiating light.
=== Historical Records === According to "Miyakojima Kijishitsugi," a mid-18th-century compilation of myths and folklore from Miyako Island, Koi-tsuno requested the Heavenly Emperor to become the island's guardian deity. The Heavenly Emperor blessed him, broke off the tip of the [[:en:Ama-no-Iwato|Ama-no-Iwato]] and cast it into the ocean, forming Miyako Island. Koi-tsuno then descended with the goddess Koitama to the site of the present-day Utaki, where they gave birth to many gods and ensured the prosperity of the island's people<ref>{{DOClink|[http://www.kisc.meiji.ac.jp/~meikodai/datebasa/miyakojima_kijisitsugi.doc Miyakojima Kijishitsugi]}} Meiji University Institute of Japanese Ancient Studies</ref>.
== History == According to "Chūdō-shi Honke Puku," the stone walls on the southern side were built by [[:en:Nakasone Toyomiya|Nakasone Toyomiya]] after his victory over [[:en:Oyake Akahachi|Oyake Akahachi]] in the [[1500 Oyake Akahachi Rebellion]], fulfilling his vow to enshrine the spirits that aided his success<ref name="shiseki">[http://www.city.miyakojima.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/miyakojimabunkazai/shiseki.html Historic Sites 1-25] Miyakojima City</ref>.
On [[December 2, 2017]], the deteriorating [[torii]] was replaced, and a Completion tier 2]][[Category: ceremony was held<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on New Torii Reconstructed for Harimizu Utaki] Miyako Shinpo, December 5, 2017</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />. * Harimizu Stone-Paved Road - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Imabayashi, Naoki (2015). "Geography, Folklore, and History of the Sakishima Islands: Miyako and Yaeyama Islands." == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] - [[Religious Beliefs in Ryukyu]]
== External Links == * [https://miyakojimabunkazai.jp/bunkazaiinfo738/ Miyakojima City Cultural Properties: Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls]

=== Creation Myth === {{See also|Miyako Island#Myths}} After Miyako Island was formed, the deities '''Koi-tsuno''' and '''Koitama''' descended from the heavens to Harimizu, accompanied by many other gods. After creating humans and all things on earth, they returned to the heavens. It is said that by establishing the Utaki and continuing worship, the island prospered and flourished<ref name="minwa">[http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/chiiki_ritou/simajima/minwa.html#15 The Story of the Islands: Miyako Island's Beginning] Okinawa Prefectural Government</ref><ref>Imabayashi (2015)</ref>.
=== Human-Serpent Marriage Legend === In Hirara, a noble couple lived but had no children. They prayed to the heavens and were granted a beautiful daughter. When she became pregnant at a young age, her parents inquired, and she revealed that a young man visited her each night. The father traced the man's whereabouts and discovered he was a giant serpent living in the cave at Harimizu Utaki.
That night, the serpent appeared in the daughter's dream, revealing himself as Koi-tsuno, the guardian deity of the island. He instructed her to bring their three daughters to the Utaki when they turned three years old. The daughters were brought there, and as they embraced the serpent without fear, he wept with joy and transformed them into guardian deities of the island. The serpent ascended to the heavens, radiating light.
=== Historical Records === According to "Miyakojima Kijishitsugi," a mid-18th-century compilation of myths and folklore from Miyako Island, Koi-tsuno requested the Heavenly Emperor to become the island's guardian deity. The Heavenly Emperor blessed him, broke off the tip of the [[:en:Ama-no-Iwato|Ama-no-Iwato]] and cast it into the ocean, forming Miyako Island. Koi-tsuno then descended with the goddess Koitama to the site of the present-day Utaki, where they gave birth to many gods and ensured the prosperity of the island's people<ref>{{DOClink|[http://www.kisc.meiji.ac.jp/~meikodai/datebasa/miyakojima_kijisitsugi.doc Miyakojima Kijishitsugi]}} Meiji University Institute of Japanese Ancient Studies</ref>.
== History == According to "Chūdō-shi Honke Puku," the stone walls on the southern side were built by [[:en:Nakasone Toyomiya|Nakasone Toyomiya]] after his victory over [[:en:Oyake Akahachi|Oyake Akahachi]] in the [[1500 Oyake Akahachi Rebellion]], fulfilling his vow to enshrine the spirits that aided his success<ref name="shiseki">[http://www.city.miyakojima.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/miyakojimabunkazai/shiseki.html Historic Sites 1-25] Miyakojima City</ref>.
On [[December 2, 2017]], the deteriorating [[torii]] was replaced, and a Completion tier 2]][[Category: ceremony was held<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on New Torii Reconstructed for Harimizu Utaki] Miyako Shinpo, December 5, 2017</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />. * Harimizu Stone-Paved Road - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Imabayashi, Naoki (2015). "Geography, Folklore, and History of the Sakishima Islands: Miyako and Yaeyama Islands." == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] - [[Religious Beliefs in Ryukyu]]
== External Links == * [https://miyakojimabunkazai.jp/bunkazaiinfo738/ Miyakojima City Cultural Properties: Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls]

It is also known as "Tsukasayā"<ref name="okinawastory">[http://www.okinawastory.jp/spot/20340905 Harimizu Utaki] Okinawa Story (Okinawa Convention & Visitors Bureau)</ref>. {{coord|24|48|26.49|N|125|16|46.58|E|scale:2500|display=title}}
== Overview == Harimizu Utaki enshrines the two deities, Koi-tsuno (古意角) and Koitama (姑依玉). It has been a site of worship since before the establishment of the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]] (1429) and is the setting for various [[:en:myths|myths]] and [[:en:legends|legends]] related to the creation of Miyako Island<ref name="okinawastory" />.
== Origin and Legends ==
=== Creation Myth === {{See also|Miyako Island#Myths}} After Miyako Island was formed, the deities '''Koi-tsuno''' and '''Koitama''' descended from the heavens to Harimizu, accompanied by many other gods. After creating humans and all things on earth, they returned to the heavens. It is said that by establishing the Utaki and continuing worship, the island prospered and flourished<ref name="minwa">[http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/chiiki_ritou/simajima/minwa.html#15 The Story of the Islands: Miyako Island's Beginning] Okinawa Prefectural Government</ref><ref>Imabayashi (2015)</ref>.
=== Human-Serpent Marriage Legend === In Hirara, a noble couple lived but had no children. They prayed to the heavens and were granted a beautiful daughter. When she became pregnant at a young age, her parents inquired, and she revealed that a young man visited her each night. The father traced the man's whereabouts and discovered he was a giant serpent living in the cave at Harimizu Utaki.
That night, the serpent appeared in the daughter's dream, revealing himself as Koi-tsuno, the guardian deity of the island. He instructed her to bring their three daughters to the Utaki when they turned three years old. The daughters were brought there, and as they embraced the serpent without fear, he wept with joy and transformed them into guardian deities of the island. The serpent ascended to the heavens, radiating light.
=== Historical Records === According to "Miyakojima Kijishitsugi," a mid-18th-century compilation of myths and folklore from Miyako Island, Koi-tsuno requested the Heavenly Emperor to become the island's guardian deity. The Heavenly Emperor blessed him, broke off the tip of the [[:en:Ama-no-Iwato|Ama-no-Iwato]] and cast it into the ocean, forming Miyako Island. Koi-tsuno then descended with the goddess Koitama to the site of the present-day Utaki, where they gave birth to many gods and ensured the prosperity of the island's people<ref>{{DOClink|[http://www.kisc.meiji.ac.jp/~meikodai/datebasa/miyakojima_kijisitsugi.doc Miyakojima Kijishitsugi]}} Meiji University Institute of Japanese Ancient Studies</ref>.
== History == According to "Chūdō-shi Honke Puku," the stone walls on the southern side were built by [[:en:Nakasone Toyomiya|Nakasone Toyomiya]] after his victory over [[:en:Oyake Akahachi|Oyake Akahachi]] in the [[1500 Oyake Akahachi Rebellion]], fulfilling his vow to enshrine the spirits that aided his success<ref name="shiseki">[http://www.city.miyakojima.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/miyakojimabunkazai/shiseki.html Historic Sites 1-25] Miyakojima City</ref>.
On [[December 2, 2017]], the deteriorating [[torii]] was replaced, and a Completion tier 2]][[Category: ceremony was held<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on New Torii Reconstructed for Harimizu Utaki] Miyako Shinpo, December 5, 2017</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />. * Harimizu Stone-Paved Road - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Imabayashi, Naoki (2015). "Geography, Folklore, and History of the Sakishima Islands: Miyako and Yaeyama Islands." == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] - [[Religious Beliefs in Ryukyu]]
== External Links == * [https://miyakojimabunkazai.jp/bunkazaiinfo738/ Miyakojima City Cultural Properties: Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:漲水御嶽]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11565602]] Wikidata name: Harimizu-utaki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox religious building |name=Pyarumizu-utaki |image=Miyako harimizu utaki.jpg }} '''Pyarumizu-utaki''' (漲水御嶽, Harimizu Utaki, [[:en:Miyako language|Miyako language]]: Piyarimizu Utaki) is an [[:en:Ontake|Utaki]] located in Hirara Nishizato, [[:en:Miyakojima, Okinawa|Miyakojima, Okinawa]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]]. It is also known as "Tsukasayā"<ref name="okinawastory">[http://www.okinawastory.jp/spot/20340905 Harimizu Utaki] Okinawa Story (Okinawa Convention & Visitors Bureau)</ref>. {{coord|24|48|26.49|N|125|16|46.58|E|scale:2500|display=title}}
== Overview == Harimizu Utaki enshrines the two deities, Koi-tsuno (古意角) and Koitama (姑依玉). It has been a site of worship since before the establishment of the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]] (1429) and is the setting for various [[:en:myths|myths]] and [[:en:legends|legends]] related to the creation of Miyako Island<ref name="okinawastory" />.
== Origin and Legends ==
=== Creation Myth === {{See also|Miyako Island#Myths}} After Miyako Island was formed, the deities '''Koi-tsuno''' and '''Koitama''' descended from the heavens to Harimizu, accompanied by many other gods. After creating humans and all things on earth, they returned to the heavens. It is said that by establishing the Utaki and continuing worship, the island prospered and flourished<ref name="minwa">[http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/chiiki_ritou/simajima/minwa.html#15 The Story of the Islands: Miyako Island's Beginning] Okinawa Prefectural Government</ref><ref>Imabayashi (2015)</ref>.
=== Human-Serpent Marriage Legend === In Hirara, a noble couple lived but had no children. They prayed to the heavens and were granted a beautiful daughter. When she became pregnant at a young age, her parents inquired, and she revealed that a young man visited her each night. The father traced the man's whereabouts and discovered he was a giant serpent living in the cave at Harimizu Utaki.
That night, the serpent appeared in the daughter's dream, revealing himself as Koi-tsuno, the guardian deity of the island. He instructed her to bring their three daughters to the Utaki when they turned three years old. The daughters were brought there, and as they embraced the serpent without fear, he wept with joy and transformed them into guardian deities of the island. The serpent ascended to the heavens, radiating light.
=== Historical Records === According to "Miyakojima Kijishitsugi," a mid-18th-century compilation of myths and folklore from Miyako Island, Koi-tsuno requested the Heavenly Emperor to become the island's guardian deity. The Heavenly Emperor blessed him, broke off the tip of the [[:en:Ama-no-Iwato|Ama-no-Iwato]] and cast it into the ocean, forming Miyako Island. Koi-tsuno then descended with the goddess Koitama to the site of the present-day Utaki, where they gave birth to many gods and ensured the prosperity of the island's people<ref>{{DOClink|[http://www.kisc.meiji.ac.jp/~meikodai/datebasa/miyakojima_kijisitsugi.doc Miyakojima Kijishitsugi]}} Meiji University Institute of Japanese Ancient Studies</ref>.
== History == According to "Chūdō-shi Honke Puku," the stone walls on the southern side were built by [[:en:Nakasone Toyomiya|Nakasone Toyomiya]] after his victory over [[:en:Oyake Akahachi|Oyake Akahachi]] in the [[1500 Oyake Akahachi Rebellion]], fulfilling his vow to enshrine the spirits that aided his success<ref name="shiseki">[http://www.city.miyakojima.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/miyakojimabunkazai/shiseki.html Historic Sites 1-25] Miyakojima City</ref>.
On [[December 2, 2017]], the deteriorating [[torii]] was replaced, and a Completion tier 2]][[Category: ceremony was held<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on New Torii Reconstructed for Harimizu Utaki] Miyako Shinpo, December 5, 2017</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />. * Harimizu Stone-Paved Road - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Imabayashi, Naoki (2015). "Geography, Folklore, and History of the Sakishima Islands: Miyako and Yaeyama Islands." == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] - [[Religious Beliefs in Ryukyu]]
== External Links == * [https://miyakojimabunkazai.jp/bunkazaiinfo738/ Miyakojima City Cultural Properties: Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls]

On [[December 2, 2017]], the deteriorating [[torii]] was replaced, and a Completion tier 2]][[Category: ceremony was held<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on New Torii Reconstructed for Harimizu Utaki] Miyako Shinpo, December 5, 2017</ref>.

== Cultural Properties == * Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />. * Harimizu Stone-Paved Road - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Imabayashi, Naoki (2015). "Geography, Folklore, and History of the Sakishima Islands: Miyako and Yaeyama Islands." == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] - [[Religious Beliefs in Ryukyu]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:漲水御嶽]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:漲水御嶽]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:漲水御嶽]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11565602]] Wikidata name: Harimizu-utaki [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:54, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{infobox religious building |name=Pyarumizu-utaki |image=Miyako harimizu utaki.jpg }} '''Pyarumizu-utaki''' (漲水御嶽, Harimizu Utaki, [[:en:Miyako language|Miyako language]]: Piyarimizu Utaki) is an [[:en:Ontake|Utaki]] located in Hirara Nishizato, [[:en:Miyakojima, Okinawa|Miyakojima, Okinawa]], [[:en:Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa Prefecture]]. It is also known as "Tsukasayā"<ref name="okinawastory">[http://www.okinawastory.jp/spot/20340905 Harimizu Utaki] Okinawa Story (Okinawa Convention & Visitors Bureau)</ref>. {{coord|24|48|26.49|N|125|16|46.58|E|scale:2500|display=title}}
== Overview == Harimizu Utaki enshrines the two deities, Koi-tsuno (古意角) and Koitama (姑依玉). It has been a site of worship since before the establishment of the [[:en:Ryukyu Kingdom|Ryukyu Kingdom]] (1429) and is the setting for various [[:en:myths|myths]] and [[:en:legends|legends]] related to the creation of Miyako Island<ref name="okinawastory" />.
== Origin and Legends ==
=== Creation Myth === {{See also|Miyako Island#Myths}} After Miyako Island was formed, the deities '''Koi-tsuno''' and '''Koitama''' descended from the heavens to Harimizu, accompanied by many other gods. After creating humans and all things on earth, they returned to the heavens. It is said that by establishing the Utaki and continuing worship, the island prospered and flourished<ref name="minwa">[http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/chiiki_ritou/simajima/minwa.html#15 The Story of the Islands: Miyako Island's Beginning] Okinawa Prefectural Government</ref><ref>Imabayashi (2015)</ref>.
=== Human-Serpent Marriage Legend === In Hirara, a noble couple lived but had no children. They prayed to the heavens and were granted a beautiful daughter. When she became pregnant at a young age, her parents inquired, and she revealed that a young man visited her each night. The father traced the man's whereabouts and discovered he was a giant serpent living in the cave at Harimizu Utaki.
That night, the serpent appeared in the daughter's dream, revealing himself as Koi-tsuno, the guardian deity of the island. He instructed her to bring their three daughters to the Utaki when they turned three years old. The daughters were brought there, and as they embraced the serpent without fear, he wept with joy and transformed them into guardian deities of the island. The serpent ascended to the heavens, radiating light.
=== Historical Records === According to "Miyakojima Kijishitsugi," a mid-18th-century compilation of myths and folklore from Miyako Island, Koi-tsuno requested the Heavenly Emperor to become the island's guardian deity. The Heavenly Emperor blessed him, broke off the tip of the [[:en:Ama-no-Iwato|Ama-no-Iwato]] and cast it into the ocean, forming Miyako Island. Koi-tsuno then descended with the goddess Koitama to the site of the present-day Utaki, where they gave birth to many gods and ensured the prosperity of the island's people<ref>{{DOClink|[http://www.kisc.meiji.ac.jp/~meikodai/datebasa/miyakojima_kijisitsugi.doc Miyakojima Kijishitsugi]}} Meiji University Institute of Japanese Ancient Studies</ref>.
== History == According to "Chūdō-shi Honke Puku," the stone walls on the southern side were built by [[:en:Nakasone Toyomiya|Nakasone Toyomiya]] after his victory over [[:en:Oyake Akahachi|Oyake Akahachi]] in the [[1500 Oyake Akahachi Rebellion]], fulfilling his vow to enshrine the spirits that aided his success<ref name="shiseki">[http://www.city.miyakojima.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/miyakojimabunkazai/shiseki.html Historic Sites 1-25] Miyakojima City</ref>.
On [[December 2, 2017]], the deteriorating [[torii]] was replaced, and a Completion tier 2]][[Category: ceremony was held<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on New Torii Reconstructed for Harimizu Utaki] Miyako Shinpo, December 5, 2017</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />. * Harimizu Stone-Paved Road - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Imabayashi, Naoki (2015). "Geography, Folklore, and History of the Sakishima Islands: Miyako and Yaeyama Islands." == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] - [[Religious Beliefs in Ryukyu]]
== External Links == * [https://miyakojimabunkazai.jp/bunkazaiinfo738/ Miyakojima City Cultural Properties: Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls]

== Origin and Legends ==
=== Creation Myth === {{See also|Miyako Island#Myths}} After Miyako Island was formed, the deities '''Koi-tsuno''' and '''Koitama''' descended from the heavens to Harimizu, accompanied by many other gods. After creating humans and all things on earth, they returned to the heavens. It is said that by establishing the Utaki and continuing worship, the island prospered and flourished<ref name="minwa">[http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/chiiki_ritou/simajima/minwa.html#15 The Story of the Islands: Miyako Island's Beginning] Okinawa Prefectural Government</ref><ref>Imabayashi (2015)</ref>.
=== Human-Serpent Marriage Legend === In Hirara, a noble couple lived but had no children. They prayed to the heavens and were granted a beautiful daughter. When she became pregnant at a young age, her parents inquired, and she revealed that a young man visited her each night. The father traced the man's whereabouts and discovered he was a giant serpent living in the cave at Harimizu Utaki.
That night, the serpent appeared in the daughter's dream, revealing himself as Koi-tsuno, the guardian deity of the island. He instructed her to bring their three daughters to the Utaki when they turned three years old. The daughters were brought there, and as they embraced the serpent without fear, he wept with joy and transformed them into guardian deities of the island. The serpent ascended to the heavens, radiating light.
=== Historical Records === According to "Miyakojima Kijishitsugi," a mid-18th-century compilation of myths and folklore from Miyako Island, Koi-tsuno requested the Heavenly Emperor to become the island's guardian deity. The Heavenly Emperor blessed him, broke off the tip of the [[:en:Ama-no-Iwato|Ama-no-Iwato]] and cast it into the ocean, forming Miyako Island. Koi-tsuno then descended with the goddess Koitama to the site of the present-day Utaki, where they gave birth to many gods and ensured the prosperity of the island's people<ref>{{DOClink|[http://www.kisc.meiji.ac.jp/~meikodai/datebasa/miyakojima_kijisitsugi.doc Miyakojima Kijishitsugi]}} Meiji University Institute of Japanese Ancient Studies</ref>.
== History == According to "Chūdō-shi Honke Puku," the stone walls on the southern side were built by [[:en:Nakasone Toyomiya|Nakasone Toyomiya]] after his victory over {{ill|Oyake Akahachi|en|Oyake Akahachi|WD=Q845103|lt=Oyake Akahachi}} in the [[1500 Oyake Akahachi Rebellion]], fulfilling his vow to enshrine the spirits that aided his success<ref name="shiseki">[http://www.city.miyakojima.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/miyakojimabunkazai/shiseki.html Historic Sites 1-25] Miyakojima City</ref>.
On [[December 2, 2017]], the deteriorating [[torii]] was replaced, and a Completion tier 2]][[Category: ceremony was held<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on New Torii Reconstructed for Harimizu Utaki] Miyako Shinpo, December 5, 2017</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />. * Harimizu Stone-Paved Road - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Imabayashi, Naoki (2015). "Geography, Folklore, and History of the Sakishima Islands: Miyako and Yaeyama Islands." == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] - [[Religious Beliefs in Ryukyu]]
== External Links == * [https://miyakojimabunkazai.jp/bunkazaiinfo738/ Miyakojima City Cultural Properties: Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls]

{{infobox religious building |name=Pyarumizu-utaki |image=Miyako harimizu utaki.jpg }} '''Pyarumizu-utaki''' (漲水御嶽, Harimizu Utaki, [[:en:Miyako language|Miyako language]]: Piyarimizu Utaki) is an {{ill|Ontake|en|Ontake|WD=Q11071176|lt=Utaki}} located in Hirara Nishizato, {{ill|Miyakojima, Okinawa|en|Miyakojima, Okinawa|WD=Q714636|lt=Miyakojima, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture}}. It is also known as "Tsukasayā"<ref name="okinawastory">[http://www.okinawastory.jp/spot/20340905 Harimizu Utaki] Okinawa Story (Okinawa Convention & Visitors Bureau)</ref>. {{coord|24|48|26.49|N|125|16|46.58|E|scale:2500|display=title}}
== Overview == Harimizu Utaki enshrines the two deities, Koi-tsuno (古意角) and Koitama (姑依玉). It has been a site of worship since before the establishment of the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|en|Ryukyu Kingdom|WD=Q28025|lt=Ryukyu Kingdom}} (1429) and is the setting for various [[:en:myths|myths]] and [[:en:legends|legends]] related to the creation of Miyako Island<ref name="okinawastory" />.
== Origin and Legends ==
=== Creation Myth === {{See also|Miyako Island#Myths}} After Miyako Island was formed, the deities '''Koi-tsuno''' and '''Koitama''' descended from the heavens to Harimizu, accompanied by many other gods. After creating humans and all things on earth, they returned to the heavens. It is said that by establishing the Utaki and continuing worship, the island prospered and flourished<ref name="minwa">[http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/chiiki_ritou/simajima/minwa.html#15 The Story of the Islands: Miyako Island's Beginning] Okinawa Prefectural Government</ref><ref>Imabayashi (2015)</ref>.
=== Human-Serpent Marriage Legend === In Hirara, a noble couple lived but had no children. They prayed to the heavens and were granted a beautiful daughter. When she became pregnant at a young age, her parents inquired, and she revealed that a young man visited her each night. The father traced the man's whereabouts and discovered he was a giant serpent living in the cave at Harimizu Utaki.
That night, the serpent appeared in the daughter's dream, revealing himself as Koi-tsuno, the guardian deity of the island. He instructed her to bring their three daughters to the Utaki when they turned three years old. The daughters were brought there, and as they embraced the serpent without fear, he wept with joy and transformed them into guardian deities of the island. The serpent ascended to the heavens, radiating light.
=== Historical Records === According to "Miyakojima Kijishitsugi," a mid-18th-century compilation of myths and folklore from Miyako Island, Koi-tsuno requested the Heavenly Emperor to become the island's guardian deity. The Heavenly Emperor blessed him, broke off the tip of the [[:en:Ama-no-Iwato|Ama-no-Iwato]] and cast it into the ocean, forming Miyako Island. Koi-tsuno then descended with the goddess Koitama to the site of the present-day Utaki, where they gave birth to many gods and ensured the prosperity of the island's people<ref>{{DOClink|[http://www.kisc.meiji.ac.jp/~meikodai/datebasa/miyakojima_kijisitsugi.doc Miyakojima Kijishitsugi]}} Meiji University Institute of Japanese Ancient Studies</ref>.
== History == According to "Chūdō-shi Honke Puku," the stone walls on the southern side were built by [[:en:Nakasone Toyomiya|Nakasone Toyomiya]] after his victory over {{ill|Oyake Akahachi|en|Oyake Akahachi|WD=Q845103|lt=Oyake Akahachi}} in the [[1500 Oyake Akahachi Rebellion]], fulfilling his vow to enshrine the spirits that aided his success<ref name="shiseki">[http://www.city.miyakojima.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/miyakojimabunkazai/shiseki.html Historic Sites 1-25] Miyakojima City</ref>.
On [[December 2, 2017]], the deteriorating [[torii]] was replaced, and a Completion tier 2]][[Category: ceremony was held<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on New Torii Reconstructed for Harimizu Utaki] Miyako Shinpo, December 5, 2017</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />. * Harimizu Stone-Paved Road - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Imabayashi, Naoki (2015). "Geography, Folklore, and History of the Sakishima Islands: Miyako and Yaeyama Islands." == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] - [[Religious Beliefs in Ryukyu]]
== External Links == * [https://miyakojimabunkazai.jp/bunkazaiinfo738/ Miyakojima City Cultural Properties: Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:漲水御嶽]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:漲水御嶽]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:漲水御嶽]]
{{infobox religious building |name=Pyarumizu-utaki |image=Miyako harimizu utaki.jpg }} '''Pyarumizu-utaki''' (漲水御嶽, Harimizu Utaki, [[:en:Miyako language|Miyako language]]: Piyarimizu Utaki) is an {{ill|Ontake|en|Ontake|WD=Q11071176|lt=Utaki|1=Ontake}} located in Hirara Nishizato, {{ill|Miyakojima, Okinawa|en|Miyakojima, Okinawa|WD=Q714636|lt=Miyakojima, Okinawa|1=Miyakojima, Okinawa}}, {{ill|Okinawa Prefecture|en|Okinawa Prefecture|WD=Q766445|lt=Okinawa Prefecture|1=Okinawa Prefecture}}. It is also known as "Tsukasayā"<ref name="okinawastory">[http://www.okinawastory.jp/spot/20340905 Harimizu Utaki] Okinawa Story (Okinawa Convention & Visitors Bureau)</ref>. {{coord|24|48|26.49|N|125|16|46.58|E|scale:2500|display=title}}
== Overview == Harimizu Utaki enshrines the two deities, Koi-tsuno (古意角) and Koitama (姑依玉). It has been a site of worship since before the establishment of the {{ill|Ryukyu Kingdom|en|Ryukyu Kingdom|WD=Q28025|lt=Ryukyu Kingdom|1=Ryukyu Kingdom}} (1429) and is the setting for various [[:en:myths|myths]] and [[:en:legends|legends]] related to the creation of Miyako Island<ref name="okinawastory" />.
== Origin and Legends ==
=== Creation Myth === {{See also|Miyako Island#Myths}} After Miyako Island was formed, the deities '''Koi-tsuno''' and '''Koitama''' descended from the heavens to Harimizu, accompanied by many other gods. After creating humans and all things on earth, they returned to the heavens. It is said that by establishing the Utaki and continuing worship, the island prospered and flourished<ref name="minwa">[http://www.pref.okinawa.jp/chiiki_ritou/simajima/minwa.html#15 The Story of the Islands: Miyako Island's Beginning] Okinawa Prefectural Government</ref><ref>Imabayashi (2015)</ref>.
=== Human-Serpent Marriage Legend === In Hirara, a noble couple lived but had no children. They prayed to the heavens and were granted a beautiful daughter. When she became pregnant at a young age, her parents inquired, and she revealed that a young man visited her each night. The father traced the man's whereabouts and discovered he was a giant serpent living in the cave at Harimizu Utaki.
That night, the serpent appeared in the daughter's dream, revealing himself as Koi-tsuno, the guardian deity of the island. He instructed her to bring their three daughters to the Utaki when they turned three years old. The daughters were brought there, and as they embraced the serpent without fear, he wept with joy and transformed them into guardian deities of the island. The serpent ascended to the heavens, radiating light.
=== Historical Records === According to "Miyakojima Kijishitsugi," a mid-18th-century compilation of myths and folklore from Miyako Island, Koi-tsuno requested the Heavenly Emperor to become the island's guardian deity. The Heavenly Emperor blessed him, broke off the tip of the [[:en:Ama-no-Iwato|Ama-no-Iwato]] and cast it into the ocean, forming Miyako Island. Koi-tsuno then descended with the goddess Koitama to the site of the present-day Utaki, where they gave birth to many gods and ensured the prosperity of the island's people<ref>{{DOClink|[http://www.kisc.meiji.ac.jp/~meikodai/datebasa/miyakojima_kijisitsugi.doc Miyakojima Kijishitsugi]}} Meiji University Institute of Japanese Ancient Studies</ref>.
== History == According to "Chūdō-shi Honke Puku," the stone walls on the southern side were built by [[:en:Nakasone Toyomiya|Nakasone Toyomiya]] after his victory over {{ill|Oyake Akahachi|en|Oyake Akahachi|WD=Q845103|lt=Oyake Akahachi|1=Oyake Akahachi}} in the [[1500 Oyake Akahachi Rebellion]], fulfilling his vow to enshrine the spirits that aided his success<ref name="shiseki">[http://www.city.miyakojima.lg.jp/kanko/bunkazai/miyakojimabunkazai/shiseki.html Historic Sites 1-25] Miyakojima City</ref>.
On [[December 2, 2017]], the deteriorating [[torii]] was replaced, and a Completion tier 2]][[Category: ceremony was held<ref>[http://miyakoshinpo.com/news.cgi?no=18320&continue=on New Torii Reconstructed for Harimizu Utaki] Miyako Shinpo, December 5, 2017</ref>.
== Cultural Properties == * Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />. * Harimizu Stone-Paved Road - Designated as a Miyakojima City Historic Site on August 29, 1974<ref name="shiseki" />.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Imabayashi, Naoki (2015). "Geography, Folklore, and History of the Sakishima Islands: Miyako and Yaeyama Islands." == See Also == * [[Ryukyuan religion|Ryukyu Shinto]] - [[Religious Beliefs in Ryukyu]]
== External Links == * [https://miyakojimabunkazai.jp/bunkazaiinfo738/ Miyakojima City Cultural Properties: Harimizu Utaki and Stone Walls]
